title: How Big Is the AI Economy?
dek: OpenAI-style bubble talk is on a low ebb, so it's a good time to ask dispassionately: how big is the AI economy right now? Exponential View's new report says $175B annualized and growing 3x faster than any IT wave before it — while the headlines fill with identity checks, agent-neutrality bills, and a memory shortage nasty enough to earn the name Ramageddon.

oneIdea.headline: The AI economy is real, big, and fast — more revenue-validated than any prior platform shift.
oneIdea.summary: Exponential View audited over 1,000 AI companies and found demand backed by realized revenue, not just future promise: $110B banked over 12 months, a $175B run rate, and growth 3x faster than any IT wave before it. CapEx is enormous — $848B this year, $2T cumulative — but quarterly revenue now exceeds CapEx depreciation, older GPUs earn yields past their six-year life, and high-AI-spend companies have grown revenue 92% faster than non-adopters. The bubble question isn't settled, but the indicators are far more positive than the discourse suggests.

gpCorner.note: AI commentary for UK general practice; not from the podcast

items.title: Whether it's a bubble or not, budget for AI tools that outlast the hype cycle
items.body: The edition's central claim is that AI revenue is real and compounding; Zitron's is that much of it is subsidised spend propping up a handful of loss-making labs. For a GP surgery the practical reading is the same from either direction. If NLW is right and the economy keeps growing, prices for the scribes and admin tools we already lean on should keep falling as token costs collapse — the edition notes a blended price drop from roughly seventeen dollars to two per million tokens. If Zitron is right and the money is being withdrawn, the vendors selling cheap subsidised AI scribes today may raise prices sharply or fold, exactly as he warns happened when Copilot moved users onto token-based billing. Either way, a surgery should avoid deep dependence on a single cut-price vendor and keep a manual fallback for note-taking.

items.title: Token-based billing is coming to clinical tools — watch the running cost
items.body: A recurring thread across the edition is the shift from flat subscriptions to per-token billing: Amazon lost its wholesale deal with Anthropic, and consumer tools are being repriced by usage. Agentic tasks, the edition notes, can burn around 1,200 times the tokens of a simple chat. For general practice this matters because an ambient scribe that transcribes and summarises a full consultation is far heavier than a one-line query. When a practice or PCN procures these tools, the question to ask is not just the monthly headline price but the cost per consultation at realistic volume, and whether that price is contractually protected against the memory-and-compute shortages the edition describes.

items.title: The hardware shortage ('Ramageddon') could quietly raise the cost of NHS IT
items.body: Less about AI clinically and more about the practice's wider kit: the edition reports memory prices up sharply, with Apple, Microsoft and Lenovo all raising prices and Micron quadrupling memory costs in a year. That feeds through to the ordinary computers, servers and devices a surgery buys. It is worth factoring a real-terms price rise into any hardware replacement plan over the next year or two, rather than assuming the usual gentle decline in the cost of PCs and storage.

the_other_side.text: This edition's lead rests on Exponential View's audit: $110bn banked over twelve months, a $175bn annualised run rate, quarterly revenue now exceeding CapEx depreciation — offered as proof that AI demand is real and realised, not merely promised. Zitron's piece attacks precisely that framing. The revenue, he argues, is largely circular: hyperscalers pour capital into their own unprofitable labs, OpenAI and Anthropic, which spend it straight back on compute, and only a fraction returns as "revenue" that is really venture subsidy recycled. He notes the hyperscalers won't disclose real AI revenues, hiding behind "vague run rates," and that their capex will outpace cashflow from Q3 2026. Citing the Bank for International Settlements, he warns the build-out serves demand that "does not exist outside of Anthropic and OpenAI" and rests on the hope that today's models will "turn into something completely different."

the_truth_beneath.text: Both men orbit one variable: whether the vast AI spend is converting into real, self-sustaining demand and returns, or only the promise of it. Both accept the capex is enormous — roughly $848bn this year, $2tn cumulative — that headline revenue is climbing fast, and that the money flows mainly through a handful of labs. The empirical question is whether that revenue is durable, unsubsidised paying demand; the observables are hyperscaler capex against free cash flow, lab losses against revenue, and whether "run rate" figures survive as audited profit. The residue is worldview: Exponential View reads rising realised revenue and GPUs earning past year six as validation, Zitron reads the same numbers as recycled subsidy propping a bubble. Whittemore is vindicated if the run rate becomes self-funding profit; Zitron if capex outruns cashflow and surfaces as writedowns. Both are polemicists with incentives — NLW bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain with a paid newsletter — and the signal is where they agree despite opposite priors. Watch whether Q3 2026 results show hyperscaler capex crossing above free cash flow.

headlines.headline: Fable's relaunch may require you to KYC
headlines.summary: Code strings in the Claude app suggest Fable usage will be credit-based and billed separately from subscriptions — and that model access will require users to submit identity documents. One string reads: "Your credits will be added once your identity is verified."

headlines.headline: Seems like the only path forward, similar to getting a gun license.
headlines.attribution: Max Weinberg, on the Fable identity-verification rumor
headlines.summary: NLW notes the comparison is fair given how the government views these models — and that Dario Amodei himself said companies called Mythos a "super weapon" that should require a gun license. Despite the pushback, NLW is confident nearly everyone will hand over ID anyway.

headlines.headline: Warner's bill would enshrine agent neutrality
headlines.summary: Senator Mark Warner is preparing a 25-page discussion draft that protects third-party agents (send your own agent to shop on Amazon) and creates a "duty of loyalty" barring agents from favoring undisclosed partners — like a travel agent secretly preferring Hilton. It only covers consumer-facing agents, not internal enterprise workflows, and isn't expected to move this year.

headlines.headline: AI agents must be accountable to the people they serve.
headlines.summary: Warner frames the draft as a step toward a federal framework that promotes innovation while protecting consumers. NLW is of two minds: watch how much liability it imposes on agent providers, but the neutrality principles will likely be welcomed by many.

headlines.headline: California cuts a deal for half-price Claude
headlines.summary: Governor Newsom announced the first statewide AI rollout: all state departments and local governments get Claude access at 50% off, plus free workforce training. Newsom cautioned that "AI should not replace the human work of government" — it should help workers move faster.

headlines.headline: Amazon loses its Anthropic sweetheart deal
headlines.summary: The Information reports Anthropic has renegotiated Amazon's wholesale, compute-hours pricing to token-based rates like every other large customer, starting next year. Amazon is now exploring cost savings by switching to OpenAI or its in-house Nova models — a sign the subsidy era is over.

headlines.headline: Meta bans coding agents to dodge the distillation trap
headlines.summary: Meta's Applied AI data-labeling team must now solve coding problems without Codex or Claude Code, for fear model outputs contaminate training data and trigger "serious escalations with partner companies." Distillation violates both labs' terms of service, so Meta is guarding against legal exposure as it builds its own MetaCode model.

headlines.headline: The more companies rely on frontier models to build internal AI, the harder it becomes to prove where the intelligence came from.
headlines.summary: Chubby's summary of the bind every AI company will face: to build a better internal coding model, you must ensure you're not accidentally training or evaluating on rival model outputs.

headlines.headline: Google capped Meta's Gemini usage in March
headlines.summary: Per the FT, Google imposed usage limits on Meta and other large customers to manage the compute crunch — restrictions that partly drove Meta to stop "token maxing" and push token efficiency. Meta had leaned on Gemini and Claude because they outperformed in-house Llama models.

headlines.headline: AWS raises Nvidia GPU rental prices 20%
headlines.summary: AWS is hiking EC2 capacity block prices by 20% for Nvidia workloads, sparing only its own Trainium chips. It's another data point in a supply-constrained inference market where reservations, not on-demand rentals, are now the norm.

headlines.headline: Falling spot GPU prices don't mean weaker demand
headlines.summary: H100 spot prices are down 40% from their May peak, but SemiAnalysis says contract prices keep rising. Their read: serious buyers are locking in term capacity for production workloads, shifting opportunistic spot usage toward committed deployment — not a demand slump.

headlines.headline: Ramageddon: memory prices become a tax on all electronics
headlines.summary: AI memory demand pushed Apple to raise prices up to 15% and Microsoft to hike Xbox prices; Lenovo says pricing will "never return to where it was last year." Micron raised prices 60% in three months, quadrupled them over a year, and is targeting 84% margins — putting it behind only Google and Nvidia among US firms.

headlines.headline: Apple asks to buy from a blacklisted Chinese chipmaker
headlines.summary: Amid the memory crunch, Apple petitioned the Trump administration to buy memory from Chinese supplier CXMT — a firm on the Pentagon's blacklist over alleged military ties. Separately, a California class action alleges Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron ran a memory cartel to inflate prices.

headlines.headline: $175B run rate, growing 3x faster than any IT wave
headlines.summary: Exponential View's State of the AI Economy report, drawn from over 1,000 companies with confidence-weighted sources and deduplicated spend, finds AI companies banked $110B over 12 months at a $175B annualized run rate. Their top line: demand is real, big, and fast.

headlines.headline: The industry now adds a billion in revenue every two days
headlines.summary: In 2023 the AI industry needed 180 days to add $1B in cumulative revenue. It's now gotten 90 times faster, adding each new billion in under two days.

headlines.headline: The compute super cycle is reviving a moribund power sector
headlines.summary: Global semiconductor revenue is projected to hit $1.5T this year, roughly double last year's $792B. US electricity generation was flat from 2008–2024; it's now growing at 150% of the historical average, reaching nine terawatt-hours per month in annual growth.

headlines.headline: The largest build-out in tech history is paying back — for now
headlines.summary: CapEx will hit $848B this year and $2T cumulatively since 2020, still mostly balance-sheet cash but increasingly debt-funded. Since Q4 last year, quarterly revenues have exceeded CapEx depreciation, though not yet the cumulative bill.

headlines.headline: Old GPUs are earning well past their six-year life
headlines.summary: A key bubble worry was that GPUs go obsolete before earning their return. The data suggests the opposite: rental yields show older GPUs delivering meaningful gains into years seven, eight, and even nine — a much healthier payback scenario.

headlines.headline: AI revenue is still just 0.42% of US GDP
headlines.summary: The IT sector is ~9.4% of US GDP; AI revenue equals only 0.42% — but it's grown 3x versus Q1 2025 and 10x versus Q1 2024. Even heavy users barely dent their P&L: Uber spends about $1.5K per engineer, leaving enormous room to grow.

headlines.headline: Agentic tasks use 1,200x the tokens of a chat
headlines.summary: The chat-to-agents transition is multiplying token use — an agentic coding task can consume around 1,200x the tokens of a chat query. Global token volumes now exceed 30 quadrillion per month, growing 14x year over year.

headlines.headline: Tokens got 8x cheaper as capability jumped
headlines.summary: From mid-2024 to mid-2026, the blended price per million tokens fell from $17 to $2 even as the capabilities index rose from 112 to 158, and intensity per request jumped from 12 to 36 tokens processed per output token. Price declines drive more use and make previously uneconomical apps viable.

headlines.headline: Value is moving up the stack toward models and apps
headlines.summary: Revenue is still concentrated in chips, but hosting is rising, model revenue is growing, and app revenue (companies like Cursor) is appearing for the first time. The app-and-model layer's share of AI revenue is up nearly 3x over the last year — even as labs push both down into infrastructure and up into apps.

headlines.headline: High-AI spenders grew revenue 92% faster
headlines.summary: Companies with no AI spend grew revenue roughly in line with nominal GDP (15–20%) over three years. Top-quartile AI spenders grew revenue more than 100% — a 92% growth differential. Meanwhile 33% of public companies now cite AI impact on earnings calls, up from ~10% in early 2023.

headlines.headline: Not enough people are emotionally prepared for if it's not a bubble
headlines.summary: NLW allows the market can still get over-exuberant, but calls this the most insightful bubble tweet ever — from OpenAI's Rune. The indicators of return on CapEx, he argues, are far more positive than the average discourse suggests.
